author: Elvira Clayton
artistdescription:
Based in Harlem, Elvira Clayton is a multidisciplinary artist whose work spans installation, performance, sculpture, and oral history. Deeply rooted in historical research, Clayton mines archives and slave-related documents to uncover hidden narratives of American slavery, bringing these forgotten stories to light to honor her own ancestors.

Born in Louisiana and raised in Texas, her diverse practice—which includes collage and assemblage—serves as a powerful bridge between archival research and contemporary visual storytelling.

sections.value:
2025 Shillim Institute/One Landscape Artist Residency Report by Elvira Clayton.

My 2025 Shillim Institute /One Landscape Artist Residency project centered on community engagement and creative collaboration through textile-related processes. Before my arrival, it was determined that I would work with a community of local women. I came to the residency interested in the cultural stories embedded within the region's traditional textile craft practices. I was particularly excited to learn about the community's quilting culture. I proposed working collectively with women through a series of workshops to create a textile- based mural utilizing local quilting aesthetics. Once on the ground at Shillim, it was decided that a weaving workshop would benefit the community most, so I had to pivot away from my original plan. Over the past 18 years, community-based projects have been part of my artistic practice, and I have developed a three-part formula that I believe is necessary for a successful outcome : • Entering Community- Introduction, invitation, and deep listening. • Engaging Community-Collective creative engagement and mutual exchange. • Exiting Community- Evaluation (were the project goals met?), if possible, a culminating event.

Entering Community : January 6th Dr. Manjusha, Pralhad, and I walked to the village behind Shillim (Bodshil) to meet Seema Chorghe. Dr. Manjusha introduced me, my purpose for being at Shillim, and invited her and other women from the village to participate in the artmaking workshops. After our visit with Seema, we walked through the village and was invited into the home of one the Shillim Forest Guards to have tea and cookies with him and his wife. Seema communicated that it was important that participation would lead to a new takeaway skill that the community would learn and could continue to practice beyond our time together. She agreed to speak to other women in her community and would let us know what day we could start the workshops. Additionally, Seema requested that our work begin at 4pm or later to accommodate the women's daily tasks, which included farming. Before we left, Seema brought out a quilt she had made. She shared stories about her community's quilt-making rituals, such as using remnants of old clothing from grandparents to make the quilted textile that a newborn would be wrapped in for the first five days of their life. She told me this sacred quilted work would remain in the family through the generations. Seeing the beautiful quilt and hearing Seema's stories got me excited about local quilting practices again, and I was determined to incorporate them into our collective work.

Engaging Community : January 8th, workshops began. Our first weaving workshop was held on January 8th at 4 pm at The House of Knowledge. Four to five women were expected to participate, but only two women and one teenage girl came: Seema, her daughter, and her sister-in-law, Darshana. In preparation for the workshops, I self-crafted and warped several looms using recycled cardboard and created two small weavings to show the women samples of what we would be making. I also showed samples of my work, which I brought to the residency. This work included two quilted art pieces and two textile sculptures from my ongoing project, 436. The workshop session lasted two and a half hours, during which time the participants created their first weavings.

January 9th : It was important that I honor Seema's request to learn a creative skill that she could carry away and continue to practice. In our second workshop, I taught the participants how to source the simple materials they needed, walked them through the process of warping their looms, and showed them how to remove their weavings from the loom. We discussed ways they could individualize their work using various embellishments and craft skills they already practiced, such as crochet. January 10th : In our third workshop, Seema and Darshana agreed to teach me their traditional quilt-making style. January 11th-13th : After sharing their cultural quilt-making skills with me, Seema and Darshana insisted that I share how I make my textile sculptures. (I showed samples of this work at the beginning of our first workshop).

January 11th : Seema and Darshana's children attended the workshops with them. Before the women and I began working on our individual textile sculptures, I set up a weaving workstation for the children. I soon realized that Seema had taught her younger daughter how to create a weaving from start to finish, and she was able to assist the other children while I focused on teaching the women. It was very satisfying to know that I had honored Seema's initial request. She was able to learn and share her new skill after two workshops.

January 13th, Final workshop : Seema, Darshana, and I completed our three sculptures in the style of my 436 project sculptures. We used sarees and embellishments sourced locally. Exiting Community, January 14th : As a thank-you and goodbye gift, Seema, Darshana, and their children invited me to lunch at their home. They also invited my cohort, Sanju, and the two young women who worked with Margie on the mapping project, Hibah and Vritti. Seema gave us a short tour of their village, including the farm and local water sources. Darshana prepared a delicious meal. Our community lunch in the village became the project's culminating event. It was an honor to be invited into their homes, and community, it is a memory that I will keep close to my heart. The project’s success would not have been possible without the help of members of the Shillim staff, some assigned to assist as interpreters and others curious and wanting to participate in the magic happening through our workshops. My One Landscape residency at Shillim Institue has been a series of wonderful interactions with both people and nature. I successfully achieved my goal of working collaboratively with a local community and have been set on a much-needed path toward healing. I am grateful for the opportunity to visit and learn about India and its beautiful people and culture.
